questions.question: A farmer plants corn on a 100-acre field instead of wheat. The expected wheat profit was \$40,000. Corn seeds cost \$10,000. What is the opportunity cost of planting corn?
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative forgone. The farmer gives up the \$40,000 profit from wheat, not the accounting cost of seeds.

questions.question: An architect uses a high-end workstation, a drafting table, and CAD software. Which factor of production do these tools represent?
questions.explanation: Capital consists of man-made aids to production, including physical tools and digital software used to create output.

questions.question: A society uses a lottery to allocate limited public housing slots. Which basic economic question is being addressed?
questions.explanation: This question concerns the distribution of goods among society. A lottery determines who receives the limited housing.

questions.question: Is studying how a national interest rate change affects total investment microeconomic or macroeconomic?
questions.explanation: Macroeconomics examines aggregate variables. Total investment is an economy-wide measure.

questions.question: Classify: 'The current rate of inflation is higher than the 10-year average.'
questions.explanation: This is a factual claim that can be verified against historical data. Positive economics deals with testable statements.

questions.question: What is indicated by a point located inside the PPF curve?
questions.explanation: Points inside the PPF mean the economy is not reaching its potential. Resources are idle or used inefficiently.

questions.question: An economy moves from (50X, 100Y) to (70X, 60Y) on its PPF. What is the opportunity cost of 1 unit of X?
questions.explanation: 40 units of Y are sacrificed to gain 20 units of X. The cost per X is 40 ÷ 20 = 2 units of Y.

questions.question: Sam produces 4 widgets per hour, Julie produces 6. Who has the absolute advantage?
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage means producing more output with the same input. Julie produces 6 versus Sam's 4.

questions.question: Cloth costs 2 Wine in Country P and 4 Wine in Country Q. Who has the comparative advantage in Wine?
questions.explanation: In Q, 1 Wine = 0.25 Cloth. In P, 1 Wine = 0.5 Cloth. Q has the lower opportunity cost for Wine.

questions.question: Meat costs 4 Veggies in Country A and 6 Veggies in Country B. What trade price benefits both?
questions.explanation: Trade is mutually beneficial when the price falls between the two opportunity costs. 5 lies between 4 and 6.

questions.question: If the price of leather rises, how will the supply curve for leather jackets be affected?
options: It will shift to the right
It will shift to the left
There will be movement along the curve
questions.explanation: Leather is an input. Higher input costs reduce supply at every price, shifting the curve leftward.

questions.question: How does a decrease in printer prices affect the demand curve for ink cartridges?
options: There is movement along the curve
questions.explanation: Printers and ink are complements. Cheaper printers increase ink demand at every price.

questions.question: How does improved manufacturing technology affect the smartphone supply curve?
options: There is movement along the curve
questions.explanation: Technology reduces production costs. Firms supply more at every price, shifting supply rightward.

questions.question: Frozen dinners have YED of -0.5. How does a 10% income increase affect demand?
questions.explanation: % change in Q = −0.5 × 10% = −5%. The negative YED indicates an inferior good.

questions.question: What is the term when quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the current price?
questions.explanation: A shortage, or excess demand, occurs when the price is below equilibrium. Buyers want more than sellers offer.

questions.question: A firm raises price 15% and total revenue decreases. Is demand elastic or inelastic?
questions.explanation: When a price increase lowers revenue, the quantity drop exceeds the price rise. PED > 1, so demand is elastic.

questions.question: How does spending a large share of income on a good affect its price elasticity?
options: It has no effect on elasticity
questions.explanation: Big-ticket items make consumers more price-sensitive. They actively seek alternatives when prices rise.

questions.question: A 5% income rise leads to a 2% rise in bread demand. Is bread a luxury or necessity?
questions.explanation: YED = 2% ÷ 5% = 0.4. A positive value below 1 indicates a necessity.

questions.question: If the price of Good A rises and demand for Good B also rises, what is their relationship?
questions.explanation: A positive XED means consumers switch from A to B when A becomes more expensive. They are substitutes.

questions.question: If the PES for a good is 0.5, is supply elastic or inelastic?
questions.explanation: A PES below 1 means quantity supplied is relatively unresponsive to price changes.

questions.question: How does high inventory availability affect the elasticity of supply?
options: It has no effect on elasticity
questions.explanation: Firms can release stored goods when prices rise. Inventory makes supply more responsive.

questions.question: In the long run, are there any fixed costs?
questions.explanation: In the long run, all factors of production can be adjusted. No costs remain fixed.

questions.question: Total cost for 10 units is \$100, for 11 units is \$115. What is the Marginal Cost of the 11th unit?
questions.explanation: MC = Change in TC ÷ Change in Q = \$15 ÷ 1 = \$15.

questions.question: What is a Technical Economy of Scale?
options: Lower interest rates for big firms
questions.explanation: Large-scale production justifies advanced machinery. Bigger machines often produce at lower unit cost.

questions.question: Which market structure has high barriers to entry and a single seller?
questions.explanation: A monopoly is the sole provider. Barriers prevent competitors from entering the market.

questions.question: At what point does a firm achieve Allocative Efficiency?
options: Where ATC is at its minimum
questions.explanation: Allocative efficiency occurs when P = MC. The value to consumers equals the cost of resources used.

questions.question: If capital and labor are substitutes, how does a rise in machinery prices affect labor demand?
questions.explanation: Firms switch to the relatively cheaper input. Higher capital costs make labor more attractive.

questions.question: What is Geographical Immobility?
options: Inability to move regions for available jobs
questions.explanation: Barriers like housing costs or family ties prevent relocation. Labor cannot move to where it is needed.

questions.question: In a four-sector economy, what is the formula for Leakages?
questions.explanation: Savings, Taxes, and Imports withdraw money from the circular flow. They do not return directly as spending.

questions.question: Are homegrown vegetables for personal use included in official GDP?
options: No, only market transactions are included
No, they are counted under Investment
questions.explanation: GDP measures formal market activity. Non-marketed household production is excluded.

questions.question: Which expenditure component includes changes in business inventories?
questions.explanation: Unsold goods added to stock are treated as investment. It represents current production held for future sale.

questions.question: In the income approach, what does Mixed Income represent?
options: Income from foreign and domestic sources
Income of self-employed and unincorporated businesses
questions.explanation: Mixed income blends labor and capital returns. It is hard to separate the owner's wages from business profit.

questions.question: Nominal GDP growth is 5% and Real GDP growth is 5%. What is the inflation rate?
questions.explanation: Real growth = Nominal growth − Inflation. If both are 5%, inflation must be zero.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of GDP per capita?
options: To measure average output per person
questions.explanation: GDP per capita divides total output by population. It allows comparison between countries of different sizes.

questions.question: A ski instructor is unemployed during the summer. What type of unemployment is this?
questions.explanation: Seasonal unemployment occurs when labor demand fluctuates by time of year. Tourism and agriculture are common examples.

questions.question: What is Demand-Pull Inflation?
options: Inflation when AD exceeds productive capacity
Inflation from a shrinking money supply
questions.explanation: Too much money chasing too few goods. Excess demand pulls the general price level upward.

questions.question: What happens to the Short-Run Phillips Curve if inflation expectations rise?
options: There is movement along the curve
questions.explanation: Higher expected inflation leads to higher wage demands. The SRPC shifts up at every unemployment level.

questions.question: What is Fiscal Drag caused by inflation?
options: Reduced spending due to high debt
Inflation pushing taxpayers into higher brackets
questions.explanation: Also called bracket creep. Rising nominal incomes increase tax burdens without real income gains.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Deficit and National Debt?
options: Deficit is yearly shortfall; debt is total accumulated
Debt is yearly shortfall; deficit is total accumulated
Deficit only applies to state governments
questions.explanation: The deficit is a flow variable for one year. National debt is the stock of all past deficits minus surpluses.

questions.question: What is Net Migration?
questions.explanation: Net migration measures the difference between people entering and leaving. It affects total population change.

questions.question: Which characteristic of money ensures it is easy to carry and move?
questions.explanation: Portability means money must be convenient to transport. Bulky currency would fail as a medium of exchange.

questions.question: A person saves \$500 in a cookie jar for emergencies. Which function of money is used?
questions.explanation: Money as a store of value preserves purchasing power for future use. The cookie jar holds wealth for later.

questions.question: What are Open Market Operations?
options: Central bank buying and selling bonds
questions.explanation: OMO controls the money supply. Buying bonds injects money; selling bonds withdraws it.

questions.question: What is the Reserve Ratio?
options: The ratio of loans to deposits
The fraction of deposits banks must hold as reserves
The interest rate on bank reserves
The ratio of profits to assets
questions.explanation: The reserve ratio limits credit creation. Banks must keep a set portion of deposits rather than lending all.

questions.question: What is the 50/30/20 Rule in personal budgeting?
options: 50% income for ten years, 30% for five, 20% now
questions.explanation: This guideline allocates after-tax income. It balances essentials, discretionary spending, and financial goals.

questions.question: What is a Negative Externality?
options: A benefit received by a third party
A cost imposed on a third party
questions.explanation: Negative externalities harm bystanders. The market price does not reflect the full social cost.

questions.question: What is a Subsidy?
options: A government payment to lower production costs
questions.explanation: Subsidies shift supply rightward. They encourage production of merit goods or support strategic industries.

questions.question: What is a Depression in economic terms?
options: A single quarter of falling GDP
questions.explanation: A depression is far more severe than a typical recession. It features extreme unemployment and deep GDP decline.

questions.question: How do Property Rights influence economic growth?
options: By providing security to invest and trade
questions.explanation: Secure ownership encourages investment. Without legal protection, the risk of losing assets deters economic activity.

questions.question: What is the primary disadvantage of Division of Labour for workers?
questions.explanation: While specialization boosts productivity, performing one small task repeatedly can reduce motivation and job satisfaction.
